stem: You have had your driving licence for 3 years. What is the legal alcohol limit for you in the Netherlands?

choices.text: 0.2 promille
0.5 promille
0.8 promille
There is no limit if you feel fine

explanation.short: Drivers with less than 5 years' experience are limited to 0.2 promille.
explanation.examTip: After 5 years the general limit of 0.5 promille applies.

stem: Your medicine has a yellow warning sticker on the box. What does this mean for driving?

choices.text: It is safe to drive at any time
The medicine can affect your driving — do not drive
You may only drive within the city
You may drive if you take half the dose

explanation.short: A yellow sticker warns the medicine impairs driving; do not drive while taking it.
explanation.examTip: Check the package leaflet or ask your pharmacist if you are unsure.

stem: You feel sleepy on a long drive. What is the safest action?

choices.text: Open a window and drive faster
Turn up the music and keep going
Take a break of at least 15 minutes, with coffee or fresh air
Take stronger painkillers to feel alert

explanation.short: A real break is the only effective answer to tiredness.
explanation.examTip: Fatigue impairs reaction time as much as alcohol — never drive on through it.

stem: For economical driving in a petrol car, you should generally shift up:

choices.text: Around 1000 rpm
Around 2000 rpm
Around 3500 rpm
Only at the red line

explanation.short: Shifting up around 2000 rpm in a petrol car (1500 rpm in diesel) is the eco-driving sweet spot.
explanation.examTip: Looking far ahead lets you roll smoothly instead of braking and accelerating.

stem: You expect to wait at a closed level crossing for more than a minute. To save fuel you should:

choices.text: Keep the engine running in neutral
Switch the engine off
Rev the engine occasionally
Switch on all lights

explanation.short: If you expect to wait longer than about a minute, switch the engine off.
explanation.examTip: Many modern cars do this automatically with a start-stop system.

stem: Which of these increases fuel consumption?

choices.text: Correctly inflated tyres
A clean, empty boot
Underinflated tyres and a roof box you do not need
Shifting up early

explanation.short: Low tyre pressure and unnecessary loads both raise consumption.
explanation.examTip: Check tyre pressure at least once a month.

stem: Your car has broken down on the shoulder of a motorway. Where do you place the warning triangle?

choices.text: Directly behind the car
About 30 metres behind the car
About 100 metres behind the car
On the roof of the car

explanation.short: On a motorway, place the triangle about 100 metres behind the vehicle.
explanation.examTip: On normal roads 30 metres is enough; always put on a high-visibility vest first.

stem: You arrive first at the scene of an accident with injuries. What do you do first?

choices.text: Move the injured people to the side of the road
Drive on; someone else will help
Make the area safe, then call 112
Take photos for insurance before doing anything else

explanation.short: Secure the scene with hazards and triangle, then call 112.
explanation.examTip: Only move injured people if they are in immediate danger (fire, traffic).

stem: After a minor collision with a parked car whose owner is not present, you must:

choices.text: Drive on if the damage looks small
Leave a note with your name, address and registration, and inform the police if needed
Wait only 5 minutes and then leave
Repair the other car yourself

explanation.short: You must always identify yourself after causing damage — leave your details if the owner is absent.
explanation.examTip: Driving away from any collision is a criminal offence (doorrijden na ongeval).

stem: An ambulance approaches from behind with siren and blue lights. You should:

choices.text: Brake hard immediately
Pull aside to the right and stop if necessary, making a clear path
Speed up so it does not have to overtake
Stay in the left lane to keep right clear

explanation.short: Make space to the right; stop if needed without blocking junctions.
explanation.examTip: Emergency vehicles with siren and blue lights always have priority.

stem: Which is the correct emergency number for police, fire and ambulance in the Netherlands?

explanation.short: 112 reaches all emergency services and is free from any phone.
explanation.examTip: Start with the location, then describe what has happened.

stem: When helping at an accident, what is the most important thing to do before approaching the victims?

choices.text: Check the damage to the cars
Make the scene safe and protect yourself (hazards, vest, triangle)
Take statements from witnesses
Move the vehicles off the road

explanation.short: Secure the scene first — an unsafe helper quickly becomes a second casualty.
explanation.examTip: Keep injured people still and warm until professional help arrives.

stem: What is the blood-alcohol limit for an experienced Dutch driver (more than 5 years licensed)?

explanation.short: Experienced drivers: 0.5 ‰ (0.22 mg/L breath). Novice drivers in their first 5 years: 0.2 ‰.
explanation.examTip: When in doubt — don't drive. Any alcohol slows reactions and judgement.

stem: Which is an early warning sign that you are too tired to keep driving?

choices.text: Yawning, drifting in the lane, missing exits
Bright sunlight
An empty fuel tank
Hot weather alone

explanation.short: Yawning, drifting, eye-strain and lapses in concentration mean it is time to stop.
explanation.examTip: Open windows and loud music are not real cures — take a real break.

stem: Your car breaks down on the hard shoulder of a motorway. After stopping safely, what is the next step?

choices.text: Stay in the car with seat belt on
Put on hazard lights, place the warning triangle ~30 m behind, get everyone out and behind the safety barrier
Open the bonnet and start checking the engine
Walk back along the carriageway to warn drivers

explanation.short: Hazards on, warning triangle behind the car, everyone out on the verge behind the barrier — then call for help.
explanation.examTip: Use the emergency lane only as a last resort; never stop there for non-emergency reasons.

stem: You are first at a road accident where someone is unconscious. What is the most important first action?

choices.text: Move the victim out of the car immediately
Make the scene safe, then call 112 and check if the person is breathing
Take photos for the insurance
Drive on so as not to block traffic

explanation.short: Secure the scene (hazards, warning triangle), call 112, then assess breathing and airway.
explanation.examTip: Move a casualty only when there is an immediate further danger such as fire.

stem: You passed your driving test 18 months ago. After dinner you have had two glasses of wine and feel fine. A friend asks for a lift home. What is the correct decision?

choices.text: Drive — the legal limit is 0.5‰ and two glasses of wine are usually safe
Drive, but only slowly and on quiet streets
Do not drive — as a beginner driver the limit is 0.2‰ and two glasses very likely exceed that
Drive and accept that any breath test would have to prove it

explanation.short: Beginner drivers (first 5 years) are bound by the 0.2‰ limit; two glasses almost certainly puts you over it.
explanation.examTip: Feeling fine is not a defence — the law sets a numerical limit you cannot self-measure.

stem: You see a red traffic light 200 metres ahead with cars queued. What is the most fuel-efficient action?

choices.text: Maintain speed and brake hard at the last moment
Take your foot off the accelerator now and let the car roll, braking only if needed near the queue
Accelerate to reach the queue sooner so you spend less time idling
Switch off the engine and coast in neutral

explanation.short: Releasing the accelerator early lets the engine slow the car for free; hard braking wastes kinetic energy.
explanation.examTip: Coasting in neutral with the engine off is unsafe — steering and brakes lose assistance.

stem: Your car loses power and you manage to stop on the hard shoulder of a motorway. What is the correct sequence of actions before stepping out?

choices.text: Step out, place the triangle 30 metres behind, then put on your vest
Switch on hazard lights, put on a high-visibility vest, then step out and place the triangle 100 metres behind
Step out, open the bonnet to signal a breakdown, then call for help
Stay in the car with the engine running until help arrives

explanation.short: On a motorway: hazards on, vest on before stepping out, triangle 100 metres behind, wait behind the barrier.
explanation.examTip: The vest must be on before you leave the car — stepping onto the hard shoulder unseen is the most dangerous moment.

stem: You are stopped at a red light. An ambulance approaches from behind with blue lights and siren. The light is still red. What should you do?

choices.text: Stay where you are — the red light forbids you from moving
Move forward through the red light at normal speed to clear the intersection
Move forward carefully past the line at low speed if it is safe, to make a path for the ambulance
Reverse to create space for the ambulance to pass on the right

explanation.short: The duty to make way for an emergency vehicle overrides the red light, but only at walking pace and only when it is safe.
explanation.examTip: Reversing is almost always wrong on an exam — you risk vehicles behind you.

stem: Your car suddenly loses power on the motorway. You manage to coast to the hard shoulder. What is the correct sequence of actions?

choices.text: Stay in the car with the engine running and call for help
Switch on hazard lights, get out via the passenger side, put on a high-visibility vest and wait behind the crash barrier
Place the warning triangle on the carriageway and stand next to the car
Open the bonnet and try to repair the car yourself in the lane

explanation.short: On a motorway hard shoulder you leave on the safer side, wear a hi-vis vest, and wait behind the barrier — not next to the car.
explanation.examTip: Warning triangles are not used on motorways in the Netherlands; rely on hazard lights and ANWB/police assistance.

stem: You arrive at an accident where a motorcyclist is lying in the road, unconscious but breathing, still wearing the helmet. What is the recommended action regarding the helmet?

choices.text: Remove the helmet immediately so they can breathe better
Leave the helmet on unless they have stopped breathing and you cannot ventilate
Take off the visor only, leave the helmet
Move the rider to the verge first

explanation.short: Removing a helmet risks worsening a spinal injury; only remove if you must reach the airway and breathing has stopped.
explanation.examTip: Call 112 first and report 'motor accident, rider unconscious, helmet still on'.

stem: On a long motorway drive at night you feel your eyes growing heavy and you have caught yourself drifting toward the lane marking. What is the safest action?

choices.text: Open a window and increase your speed to stay alert
Drink coffee and continue without stopping
Take the next exit or service area and stop for a real break or short sleep
Turn up the music loudly and push on to your destination

explanation.short: Drifting and heavy eyelids are late warning signs of microsleeps — only a real break or short sleep restores safe alertness.
explanation.examTip: Coffee and air help briefly but do not undo fatigue; plan a stop every two hours on long trips.

stem: You collect a prescription with a yellow warning sticker. You feel fine and are due to drive home 30 minutes later. What is the correct decision?

choices.text: Drive as normal because you feel fine
Drive only on quiet roads
Do not drive — a yellow sticker means the medicine may impair driving ability; arrange another way home
Drive only if you take half the dose

explanation.short: Yellow stickers on Dutch medication packaging warn the medicine can affect driving. Feeling fine is not proof that reaction time and judgement are unaffected.
explanation.examTip: Driving under the influence of impairing medication can be treated as drug-driving — the legal consequences match those for illegal drugs.

stem: You drive through town and see the traffic light 150 m ahead turn red while several cars queue under it. To save fuel, what is the most efficient choice?

choices.text: Hold your speed and brake firmly close to the queue
Lift off the throttle early and let engine braking carry you, timing arrival as the queue moves
Press the clutch and coast in neutral
Accelerate to overtake the queue

explanation.short: Lifting off in gear cuts fuel injection on most modern engines. Timing your arrival as the queue starts moving avoids a full stop and a hard restart.
explanation.examTip: Coasting in neutral does NOT save fuel on modern petrol cars — leave it in gear off the throttle instead.

stem: Your engine cuts out and you coast onto the motorway hard shoulder. After stopping safely, what is the correct order of next actions?

choices.text: Stay in the car with the seatbelt on and call 112
Hazard lights on, passengers out via the kerb side, everyone behind the barrier, then call assistance
Put the warning triangle behind the car first, even before passengers exit
Open the bonnet to cool the engine, then call

explanation.short: Get people to safety behind the crash barrier before anything else. A warning triangle is not mandatory on Dutch motorways and you should never walk into the lanes to place one.
explanation.examTip: On Dutch motorways the ANWB and police advise: out of the car, behind the barrier, then phone — in that order.

stem: On a narrow one-way street with cars parked on both sides, an ambulance with siren and blue lights approaches from behind. There is no obvious place to pull in. What should you do?

choices.text: Stop in the middle of the street and put on hazard lights
Continue at your speed until a junction or wider section where you can pull aside, indicating clearly
Reverse to the previous junction so the ambulance can overtake
Mount the pavement to clear the lane

explanation.short: Keep moving safely until you can pull aside without endangering pedestrians, parked cars or yourself. Stopping in the middle of a narrow street blocks the ambulance.
explanation.examTip: Mounting the pavement may injure pedestrians and is forbidden — make space lawfully where possible.

stem: You arrive first at an accident where a motorcyclist lies on the road, breathing but unconscious and still wearing a full-face helmet. What is the safest first action?

choices.text: Remove the helmet immediately so the airway is fully clear
Leave the helmet on, secure the scene, call 112, and only remove it if breathing stops
Sit the motorcyclist up against a kerb
Move them to the verge before doing anything else

explanation.short: Removing a helmet from a breathing casualty risks aggravating neck injuries. Leave it on, secure the scene, and call 112 — only remove if breathing stops and you cannot otherwise help.
explanation.examTip: Dutch first-aid (EHBO) guidance: do not move a casualty unless they are in immediate danger from fire, traffic, or water.
